4.3.a.info:
DORA is the four-step exchange every DHCP client uses to obtain an IP lease from a DHCP server: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge. The client starts with no IP address at all, so the first two steps rely heavily on Layer 2 broadcasts to find any available server.

Step-by-step: (1) Discover — client broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER (src 0.0.0.0, dst 255.255.255.255). (2) Offer — one or more servers respond with DHCPOFFER containing a proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, and lease time. (3) Request — client broadcasts DHCPREQUEST naming the chosen server (so other servers know to release their offers). (4) Acknowledge — chosen server sends DHCPACK finalizing the lease; client can now use the IP.

Exam traps: Discover and Request are BOTH broadcasts — easy to forget. The server's Offer and Ack can be unicast or broadcast depending on whether the client set the broadcast flag. Because Discover is a broadcast, it will not cross a router boundary without ip helper-address relay.

  • Src/Dst IP during Discover: 0.0.0.0 -> 255.255.255.255
  • UDP ports throughout: client 68, server 67
  • If DORA fails, Windows assigns APIPA 169.254.x.x/16

hack.memory: DORA like Dora the Explorer: Discovers, gets Offered help, Requests it, is Acknowledged. Discover + Request are broadcasts. Client=68, Server=67 (lower port = server, easy to flip).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, switch to Simulation mode, run 'ipconfig /release' then 'ipconfig /renew' on a PC. Click each of the 4 packets and verify source/dest IP, ports, and whether it's broadcast or unicast.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. DORA is on nearly every CCNA exam — know the order, the broadcasts, and the ports cold.

4.3.a.info:
DHCPDISCOVER is the first packet a DHCP client ever sends. Because the client has no IP yet, it uses 0.0.0.0 as the source IP and 255.255.255.255 as the destination (limited broadcast). The Ethernet frame destination is ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff.

Ports are the opposite of what most people expect: the client sources from UDP 68 and destines UDP 67. The client embeds its own MAC address in the chaddr (client hardware address) field so the server can respond back to it even before it has an IP.

Why it matters: Because Discover is a broadcast, it is scoped to a single broadcast domain. Any DHCP server on a different subnet will never see this packet unless a DHCP relay (ip helper-address) converts the broadcast into a unicast toward the server. This single fact drives most DHCP troubleshooting in real networks.

Src IP: 0.0.0.0
Dst IP: 255.255.255.255
Src Port: UDP 68 (client)
Dst Port: UDP 67 (server)
L2 Dst: ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff

hack.memory: Discover = 'Is anybody out there?' shouted to the whole subnet. Client has no IP, so src=0.0.0.0, dst=255.255.255.255. Client port 68, server port 67.
hack.practice: Capture a DHCPDISCOVER in Wireshark or Packet Tracer simulation mode. Verify src=0.0.0.0, dst=255.255.255.255, UDP 68->67, and the client's MAC in chaddr.
hack.meta: CCNA loves asking the source IP of a DHCP Discover. Answer: 0.0.0.0. Never 127.0.0.1. Never the APIPA address.

4.3.a.info:
DHCPOFFER is the server's reply with a proposed lease. It contains the offered IP, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, domain name, lease duration, and any other options the server is configured to hand out (NTP, TFTP, option 43 for WLCs, option 150 for phones, etc.).

The server sources from UDP 67 and destines UDP 68. The destination IP can be the offered IP as a unicast, or a broadcast if the client set the broadcast flag in the Discover (some older clients cannot accept a unicast before they have an IP bound to their NIC).

If multiple DHCP servers respond, the client will usually accept the first Offer it receives. The other servers' offered addresses remain reserved briefly until the client's Request identifies the chosen server, at which point the non-chosen servers release their offer.

nodes: Offer: IP + mask + gw + DNS + lease

hack.memory: Offer = the server's business card: IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease. UDP 67 -> 68. Broadcast flag decides if it comes as unicast or broadcast.
hack.practice: On a Cisco DHCP server, run 'show ip dhcp pool' — the 'offered' counter increments each time an Offer is sent. Generate a renewal and watch the counter.
hack.meta: The Offer packet carries all the DHCP options — know what's inside it: the Big 4 (IP/mask/gw/DNS) plus lease time.

4.3.a.info:
DHCPREQUEST is broadcast by the client to formally accept one specific Offer. Even though the client now knows which server it wants to lease from, it still broadcasts the Request so every DHCP server on the subnet hears it. This lets the non-chosen servers release the IPs they had offered back into their pools.

The Request includes a Server Identifier option naming the chosen server, and a Requested IP Address option specifying which offered IP the client wants to keep.

Request is also the packet used during renewal at T1 (50% of lease), this time as a unicast directly to the original server — no Discover/Offer needed, just Request/Ack.

hack.memory: Request = 'I'll take this one, everyone else can stop holding' — that's why it is still broadcast. During renewal, Request becomes unicast to the one server.
hack.practice: In simulation mode, watch the Request packet and open the DHCP options — confirm Server Identifier and Requested IP are present.
hack.meta: Exam trap: 'Which DHCP message is broadcast by the client AFTER receiving an Offer?' Answer: Request. Not Discover again.

4.3.a.info:
DHCPACK is the server's final confirmation that the lease is granted. After the Ack arrives, the client installs the IP, mask, gateway, and DNS; starts the T1 renewal timer at 50% of the lease; and begins forwarding traffic through its newly configured default gateway.

If the server cannot fulfill the Request (e.g., the IP is no longer available because another client grabbed it first), it sends a DHCPNAK instead, forcing the client back to square one with a new Discover.

Before accepting the Ack, many clients send a gratuitous ARP to verify no other host on the subnet is already using the offered IP. If a duplicate is detected, the client sends a DHCPDECLINE and restarts DORA.

hack.memory: Ack = 'It's yours, go use it.' Lease timer starts the moment Ack arrives. If anything is wrong, server sends NAK and DORA restarts.
hack.practice: 'show ip dhcp binding' on the server after an Ack — you'll see the active lease with the client's MAC, assigned IP, and lease expiration.
hack.meta: Know the four-step sequence by letter: D-O-R-A. The final A = Acknowledge (not Accept, not Allocate).

4.3.a.info:
ip helper-address [server-ip] is configured on the client-facing router interface (the SVI or routed port that is the client's default gateway). It tells the router: 'When you see a DHCP broadcast on this interface, convert it to a unicast and forward it to this server IP.'

The router also populates the GIADDR (Gateway IP Address) field in the DHCP packet with its own interface IP. The DHCP server uses GIADDR to know which pool to draw from — it matches GIADDR against the pool's network statement to pick the right subnet.

You can configure multiple ip helper-address commands on the same interface to point at multiple DHCP servers for redundancy. The router will forward to all of them simultaneously. Beyond DHCP, ip helper-address also forwards eight other UDP protocols by default (TFTP, DNS, time, NetBIOS, etc.) — which is sometimes surprising.

interface Vlan10
 ip address 10.10.10.1 255.255.255.0
 ip helper-address 10.1.1.100
 ip helper-address 10.1.1.101

hack.memory: Helper goes on the CLIENT side, points at the SERVER's IP. GIADDR = the router's interface IP = tells the server which pool to use.
hack.practice: Build a two-subnet topology. Client on VLAN 10, server on VLAN 20. Without helper: fails. Add 'ip helper-address' on the VLAN 10 SVI pointing at the server IP. Works immediately.
hack.meta: The #1 DHCP troubleshooting command. Exam loves asking 'where do you put ip helper-address?' Answer: on the client-facing interface.

4.3.a.info:
A DHCP relay agent is any Layer 3 device (router or L3 switch) that forwards DHCP messages between subnets. Without a relay, DHCP simply does not work across a Layer 3 boundary because Discover/Request are broadcasts and broadcasts don't cross routers.

On Cisco IOS, the relay is enabled by configuring ip helper-address on the client-facing interface. The relay does four things on each client broadcast: (1) catches the broadcast on the client side, (2) inserts its own IP into the GIADDR field, (3) changes the destination to the configured server IP, (4) forwards it as a unicast.

On the return path, the server unicasts the Offer/Ack back to the GIADDR, and the relay broadcasts (or unicasts) it onto the client's subnet so the client can receive it.

hack.memory: Relay = the translator between broadcast land and the far-away server. No relay = no DHCP across subnets.
hack.practice: Remove the helper-address and watch DORA fail in simulation mode — the broadcast dies at the router. Add it back, watch DORA succeed.
hack.meta: Concept vs command: a 'DHCP relay' is the function; 'ip helper-address' is the Cisco command that enables it.

4.3.b.info:
ip dhcp pool [name] is a global configuration command that creates a named DHCP address pool and drops you into DHCP pool configuration mode (prompt changes to (dhcp-config)#). Every pool on a router needs a unique name — the name is local to the router and has no protocol meaning.

Inside the pool you configure the subnet to serve (network), the gateway (default-router), DNS (dns-server), optional domain name, and the lease time. The pool activates as soon as the router has an interface with an IP inside the pool's network range.

A single router can host many pools — one per LAN subnet. The router automatically picks the correct pool based on the receiving interface's IP (or GIADDR if the request came through a relay).

Router(config)# ip dhcp pool LAN10
Router(dhcp-config)# network 192.168.10.0 /24
Router(dhcp-config)# default-router 192.168.10.1
Router(dhcp-config)# dns-server 8.8.8.8
Router(dhcp-config)# lease 0 8 0

hack.memory: Pool = a named bucket of IPs with settings attached. The name is just a label — the 'network' statement is what ties it to a subnet.
hack.practice: Build two pools on one router for two different LANs. Verify each LAN gets addresses from its correct pool using 'show ip dhcp binding.'
hack.meta: The first command you type when turning a router into a DHCP server. Know the prompt change it triggers.

4.3.b.info:
Inside a DHCP pool, network [subnet] [mask] declares the range of IP addresses the pool can hand out. For example, network 192.168.10.0 255.255.255.0 makes all usable host addresses in 192.168.10.0/24 available for lease.

The router will not hand out the network address (.0) or the directed broadcast (.255). To reserve additional addresses (like the gateway itself, static servers, or printers), use ip dhcp excluded-address globally — that command lives OUTSIDE pool config mode.

You can also use prefix-length notation: network 192.168.10.0 /24.

hack.memory: 'network' = what SUBNET the pool serves. Excluded addresses are separate — they go OUTSIDE the pool.
hack.practice: Create a pool for 192.168.10.0/24, exclude 192.168.10.1-10, and verify clients get .11 or higher.
hack.meta: Exam rarely tests the command syntax directly, but it will show you a config and ask what address range clients will receive.

4.3.b.info:
default-router [ip] inside the pool sets the default gateway sent to clients. This is the single most commonly missed DHCP command name — many students type default-gateway and get a syntax error.

The rule: on a Cisco device, the command for telling CLIENTS about their gateway is default-router. The command for the device itself to have a gateway (relevant only in switch or legacy contexts) is ip default-gateway. They are not interchangeable.

You can specify up to eight gateway IPs for redundancy; clients typically use the first one. Always make sure the default-router IP is inside the pool's network range, and also ip dhcp excluded-address it so DHCP doesn't try to hand out the same IP to a host.

hack.memory: Cisco DHCP: default-ROUTER (not gateway). Think 'the ROUTER is what does the routing, so the router is the default.'
hack.practice: Type 'default-gateway' inside a pool intentionally — see the error. Then type 'default-router' — success. Muscle memory.
hack.meta: Classic trap question. Answer is ALWAYS default-router inside a DHCP pool.

4.3.b.info:
dns-server [ip] [ip2...] sets the DNS servers sent to clients via DHCP option 6. You can list multiple, space-separated — clients use them in order.

Without this command, clients still get an IP and gateway but cannot resolve names. This is a common 'DHCP works but browsing is broken' scenario in labs and real networks.

Related options: domain-name example.com (option 15) appends a search domain, so a hostname like web is auto-completed to web.example.com.

hack.memory: No dns-server = IP works but no names resolve. Classic 'internet is broken' help-desk symptom.
hack.practice: Remove the dns-server command, renew a client, and try pinging an IP vs a name. IP works, name fails.
hack.meta: Option 6 (DNS) is tested conceptually — 'what does DHCP provide besides an IP?' DNS is in the core set.

4.3.b.info:
lease [days] [hours] [minutes] sets how long a client can hold an IP before renewal. Default is 1 day (24 hours). You can also use lease infinite for permanent leases (rare — used for printers, IP phones, or specific reservations).

Lease length is a trade-off: long leases reduce DHCP traffic and keep devices stable but slow IP reclamation; short leases reclaim addresses quickly but generate more DORA traffic. Wireless networks often use shorter leases (4–8 hours) because clients roam in and out.

The renewal timers T1 and T2 are always derived from the lease: T1 = 50% of lease, T2 = 87.5% of lease.

hack.memory: Default lease = 24 hours. 'lease 0 8 0' = 8 hours. 'lease infinite' = permanent (save for reservations).
hack.practice: Set 'lease 0 0 2' (2 minutes) and watch renewals fire in Packet Tracer simulation.
hack.meta: Know the default (1 day) and that T1/T2 are always fractions of the lease.

4.3.b.info:
ip dhcp excluded-address [start] [end] is a global command (NOT inside pool config) that tells the router 'never hand these IPs out via DHCP.' Use it to reserve the gateway, DNS servers, static hosts, and management IPs that live inside the pool's network range.

You can specify a single address or a range. Multiple excluded-address statements can exist; they accumulate.

Without exclusions, the router will happily lease out .1 to a PC, and now your gateway IP is in conflict. Always configure exclusions BEFORE enabling the pool.

Router(config)# ip dhcp excluded-address 192.168.10.1 192.168.10.10
Router(config)# ip dhcp pool LAN10
Router(dhcp-config)# network 192.168.10.0 /24
Router(dhcp-config)# default-router 192.168.10.1

hack.memory: excluded-address = GLOBAL command, not inside pool. Reserve gateway + servers before enabling the pool.
hack.practice: Forget to exclude the gateway, let a PC grab it, watch everything break. Now exclude and reconfigure — fixed.
hack.meta: The 'global vs pool-config' distinction is a common exam trap. Remember: excluded-address lives OUTSIDE pool mode.

4.3.c.info:
T1 is the first renewal timer, fired at 50% of the lease duration. When T1 expires, the client sends a unicast DHCPREQUEST directly to the server that granted the original lease — no Discover, no broadcast, no fuss.

If the server responds with a DHCPACK, the lease is extended and all timers reset. This is the happy path — over 95% of renewals succeed at T1 in healthy networks, and the client generates almost zero extra DHCP traffic.

If the original server is down or unreachable, T1 renewal fails silently and the client waits until T2 (87.5%) to escalate.

hack.memory: T1 = 50% = polite unicast to the original server. Think 'half-way check-in.'
hack.practice: Set a 2-minute lease in Packet Tracer and watch T1 fire around the 1-minute mark.
hack.meta: Exam: 'When does a DHCP client first attempt renewal?' 50% of lease. 'How?' Unicast Request.

4.3.c.info:
T2 is the escalation timer, fired at 87.5% of the lease duration. T2 only triggers if the T1 unicast renewal failed. At T2, the client sends a broadcast DHCPREQUEST so that ANY DHCP server on the subnet can respond — not just the original one.

This is the rebinding phase because the client is effectively saying 'my original server is gone, can anyone else confirm this lease?' If a different DHCP server has the lease in its database (for example in a failover pair), it can send an Ack and the client is rebound to that server.

If T2 also fails, the client waits until the lease expires at 100% and then releases the IP and restarts DORA from scratch.

hack.memory: T2 = 87.5% = 'original server is dead, anyone can help me.' Broadcast, not unicast.
hack.practice: Block the original server's replies mid-lease and watch T2 broadcast fire. A backup server can respond.
hack.meta: 87.5% is an oddly specific number — memorize as 7/8 of the lease.

4.3.c.info:
If both T1 and T2 renewal attempts fail and the lease reaches 100% expiration, the client MUST stop using the IP. The NIC is de-configured: no IP, no gateway, no DNS.

The client immediately restarts DORA from scratch with a new Discover. On Windows, if no server responds, the OS assigns an APIPA address (169.254.x.x/16) — a giveaway in ipconfig that DHCP has failed entirely.

During the outage window (from lease expiration until a new Ack), the host has no network connectivity. This is why lease lengths are tuned — too short means more renewal traffic and more outage risk; too long means stale addresses linger.

hack.memory: 100% = game over. Release IP, start DORA, fall back to APIPA if nothing answers.
hack.practice: Disable your DHCP server and wait for a client's lease to fully expire — watch it pick up 169.254.x.x.
hack.meta: Seeing 169.254 in ipconfig is the #1 help-desk DHCP failure indicator.

4.3.c.info:
The two renewal phases use different Layer 2/3 behaviors: T1 renewal is UNICAST directly to the original server's IP. T2 rebinding is BROADCAST so any DHCP server can respond.

This distinction matters because a unicast can cross Layer 3 boundaries without a relay — the router simply routes it normally. A broadcast, however, stays on the local subnet unless ip helper-address is configured.

So in relayed environments, T1 renewal works even when the DHCP relay is down (the client unicasts through normal routing), but T2 rebinding needs the relay to be up to reach a server in another subnet.

right.label: T2 (87.5%)
items: Needs relay to reach remote server

hack.memory: T1 = unicast = direct. T2 = broadcast = 'help, anyone!'
hack.practice: In simulation mode, verify T1 packet destination is the server IP (unicast) and T2 destination is 255.255.255.255 (broadcast).
hack.meta: Exam often asks which renewal is unicast vs broadcast — T1 = unicast, T2 = broadcast.

4.3.d.info:
An A record (Address record) maps a hostname to an IPv4 address. It is the most fundamental and most common DNS record type. Example: www.cisco.com -> 72.163.4.185.

A single hostname can have multiple A records (used for round-robin load balancing), and a single IPv4 address can be referenced by multiple hostnames.

Each A record has a TTL (Time To Live) value that tells resolvers how long to cache the answer. Short TTLs (60 seconds) mean changes propagate fast but increase DNS load; long TTLs (24 hours) are efficient but slow to change.

example.com.    300    IN    A    93.184.216.34

hack.memory: A = Address = IPv4 (32 bits). Most common record. TTL controls how long answers are cached.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup -type=A cisco.com' — note the IPv4 result.
hack.meta: Exam: 'Which record maps hostname to IPv4?' = A. Easy recall, tested often.

4.3.d.info:
An AAAA record (pronounced 'quad-A') maps a hostname to an IPv6 address. The name 'quad-A' is a memory hook: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits = 4x larger than IPv4's 32 bits, so the record name is four A's.

AAAA records coexist with A records on the same hostname. When a client supports both stacks, it typically prefers the AAAA (IPv6) answer over the A (IPv4) answer, per the happy-eyeballs algorithm.

example.com.    300    IN    AAAA    2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946

hack.memory: AAAA = quad-A = 4x bigger than A = IPv6. Memorize the '4x' relationship.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup -type=AAAA google.com' — see the IPv6 answer.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question: AAAA = IPv6, period.

4.3.d.info:
A CNAME record (Canonical Name) creates an alias — it maps one hostname to another hostname (not to an IP). When a client queries the alias, the DNS resolver returns the CNAME pointer, then resolves the canonical name to its A/AAAA record.

Example: www.example.com CNAME -> example.com. A query for www.example.com returns both the CNAME pointer AND the A record of example.com, so the client can go straight to the IP.

Rule: A CNAME cannot coexist with any other record for the same name. You cannot have both a CNAME and an A record on www.example.com — it must be one or the other. This is the most common DNS config mistake.

hack.memory: CNAME = Canonical Name = alias = 'go ask this other name.' Cannot coexist with A/AAAA on the same name.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup www.github.com' — you'll often see a CNAME chain before the final A record.
hack.meta: CNAME = alias to another NAME (not an IP). Common mistake: pointing a CNAME at an IP — illegal.

4.3.d.info:
An MX record (Mail Exchange) specifies which mail server receives email for a domain. MX records are queried by sending mail servers to know where to deliver messages addressed to user@example.com.

Each MX record has a priority value (lower = more preferred). A domain can have multiple MX records for redundancy: the sender tries the lowest-priority server first and falls back to higher-priority ones if it's unreachable.

example.com.    300    IN    MX    10 mail1.example.com.
example.com.    300    IN    MX    20 mail2.example.com.

In the example above, mail1 (priority 10) is tried first; mail2 (priority 20) is the backup.

hack.memory: MX = Mail eXchange. Priority: LOWER = BETTER (like golf). Primary first, backup second.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup -type=MX gmail.com' — see multiple MX records with priorities.
hack.meta: Exam: 'Which record identifies a mail server?' = MX. Priority is a detail sometimes tested.

4.3.d.info:
A PTR record (Pointer) provides reverse DNS — it maps an IP address back to a hostname. PTR records live in special reverse zones: in-addr.arpa for IPv4 and ip6.arpa for IPv6.

Example: the PTR for 8.8.8.8 is stored at 8.8.8.8.in-addr.arpa and returns dns.google.

Why it matters: Mail servers use PTR records to check whether an incoming SMTP connection has valid reverse DNS — this helps filter spam. Many mail servers reject mail from IPs that have no PTR record. Traceroute output also uses PTRs to show hostnames alongside IPs.

hack.memory: PTR = PoinTeR = IP to name (reverse). A = name to IP (forward). They are INVERSES.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup 8.8.8.8' — the answer is a PTR lookup returning dns.google.
hack.meta: PTR = reverse DNS. If a question says 'IP -> hostname,' answer is PTR.

4.3.d.info:
An NS record (Name Server) specifies which DNS servers are authoritative for a zone. NS records live at both the parent zone (delegation) and inside the zone itself (apex).

When a resolver follows the DNS hierarchy, the .com TLD server returns NS records pointing to the authoritative servers for example.com. The resolver then queries those servers for the actual record (A, MX, etc.).

example.com.    172800    IN    NS    ns1.example.com.
example.com.    172800    IN    NS    ns2.example.com.

hack.memory: NS = Name Server = 'these are the servers that know about this zone.' Delegation happens via NS.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup -type=NS cisco.com' — see the list of authoritative servers.
hack.meta: Lightly tested at CCNA level. Just know: NS = who is authoritative for the zone.

4.3.d.info:
A SOA record (Start of Authority) sits at the top of every DNS zone and contains zone administration metadata: the primary authoritative nameserver, the admin contact email (with @ replaced by a dot), the serial number, and the refresh/retry/expire/minimum TTL timers.

Secondary DNS servers compare their SOA serial number to the primary's. If the primary's serial has incremented, the secondary triggers a zone transfer to pull the updated records.

example.com. 3600 IN SOA ns1.example.com. admin.example.com. (
  2026041601 ; serial (YYYYMMDDnn)
  7200       ; refresh
  3600       ; retry
  1209600    ; expire
  3600 )     ; minimum TTL

hack.memory: SOA = Start Of Authority = zone metadata. Serial number drives secondary refresh.
hack.practice: Run 'dig example.com SOA' and identify each field.
hack.meta: At CCNA depth, just know: SOA = zone admin/metadata record, exists once per zone.

4.3.e.info:
A recursive query asks a DNS server to do ALL the work and return a single final answer. The client sends one query and expects either the resolved IP address or an error — no referrals.

Almost all client-to-local-resolver queries are recursive. The client (your PC) is 'lazy' — it just asks its configured DNS server (e.g., 8.8.8.8 or 192.168.1.1) 'give me the IP for www.cisco.com' and waits for the final answer. The local resolver does all the climbing through the DNS hierarchy on the client's behalf.

Recursive queries put the workload on the DNS server, which is why public resolvers (Google 8.8.8.8, Cloudflare 1.1.1.1) cache aggressively — otherwise they'd be overwhelmed.

hack.memory: Recursive = 'YOU do the work, give me THE answer.' Lazy client, busy resolver.
hack.practice: 'nslookup www.cisco.com' — one query, one answer. You just made a recursive request.
hack.meta: Exam: 'Which query type expects the DNS server to return the final answer?' = Recursive.

4.3.e.info:
An iterative query returns the best answer the server currently has — which may be a referral to another server rather than the final answer. The querying device is responsible for following up with the referred server.

Iterative queries are how DNS servers talk to each other. When your local resolver needs to resolve www.cisco.com, it iteratively queries: (1) a root server, gets a referral to .com TLD; (2) .com TLD, gets a referral to Cisco's authoritative servers; (3) Cisco's servers, gets the final answer.

The root and TLD servers refuse to do recursion — they would drown in traffic. They only provide referrals. This is why DNS scales to billions of queries per day.

hack.memory: Iterative = 'Here's a hint, go ask them.' Cooperative referrals. Root/TLD servers ONLY do iterative.
hack.practice: 'dig +trace cisco.com' — shows every iterative step from root down to authoritative.
hack.meta: Root servers only answer iteratively. Memorize that pattern.

4.3.e.info:
A normal DNS lookup involves both query types in sequence:

  1. Client -> Local resolver: RECURSIVE. The PC sends one recursive query and waits.
  2. Local resolver -> Root: ITERATIVE. Root returns a referral to the .com TLD servers.
  3. Local resolver -> .com TLD: ITERATIVE. TLD returns a referral to example.com's authoritative servers.
  4. Local resolver -> Authoritative: ITERATIVE. Authoritative returns the A record.
  5. Local resolver -> Client: Final answer returned. The resolver also caches the answer per the TTL.

The client only ever makes ONE query (the recursive one). All the iterative legwork happens inside the resolver, invisibly.

hack.memory: Client always recursive (lazy). Resolver is iterative (climbs tree). One + many = answer.
hack.practice: Clear your OS DNS cache ('ipconfig /flushdns' on Windows) and run a lookup — you just forced a full resolution.
hack.meta: Know that the client sends ONE recursive query. Resolver hides all iterative work.

4.3.e.info:
DNS caching stores resolved answers temporarily so repeat queries don't require a full resolution. Caching happens at multiple layers: browser cache, OS cache, local resolver cache, and sometimes enterprise DNS appliance cache.

Each record includes a TTL (Time To Live) set by the authoritative server. Resolvers keep the answer cached for exactly TTL seconds, then discard it and re-query. Common TTLs: 300 sec (5 min) for volatile records, 86400 sec (1 day) for stable records.

Caching is what makes DNS scale. Without it, every web click would require a full root->TLD->auth chain. With it, 80%+ of queries are served from cache within milliseconds.

hack.memory: TTL = cache lifetime. Short TTL = fast change propagation. Long TTL = efficient but slow to update.
hack.practice: 'ipconfig /displaydns' on Windows shows cached entries and remaining TTLs.
hack.meta: Caching is a conceptual topic — know WHY it exists (scale, speed) and that TTL drives it.

4.3.f.info:
The DNS namespace is a tree: the root (.) at the top, then TLDs (.com, .org, country codes like .uk), then second-level domains (example.com, cisco.com), then hosts/subdomains (www.example.com, mail.example.com).

Names are read RIGHT-TO-LEFT in DNS terms — www.cisco.com means: root (implied trailing dot), then .com TLD, then cisco.com, then the www host. Each level is managed by different entities: IANA runs the root, registries like Verisign run .com, domain owners run their own zones.

Resolution path: every lookup conceptually starts at the root and walks down through referrals. In practice, cached results short-circuit most queries before they reach the root.

hack.memory: Read RIGHT to LEFT: . -> .com -> cisco.com -> www.cisco.com. Each level delegates to the next.
hack.practice: Trace the resolution of www.cisco.com on paper: root refers to .com, .com refers to cisco.com, cisco.com gives the IP.
hack.meta: Know the order: root -> TLD -> 2nd level -> host. Classic CCNA recall question.

4.3.f.info:
There are 13 named root server clusters, lettered A through M (a.root-servers.net through m.root-servers.net). The number 13 comes from the original design constraint: all root NS records had to fit in a single 512-byte UDP DNS response.

Each 'root server' is actually a globally distributed anycast cluster of hundreds of physical servers sharing the same IP address. When you query a.root-servers.net, BGP anycast routes your packet to the nearest instance — which might be 10ms away in your city instead of halfway around the world.

Root servers only answer iterative queries for TLD referrals. They never return A records for individual domains.

hack.memory: 13 letters (A-M), anycast = many physical servers behind each letter. Iterative only.
hack.practice: Run 'dig . NS' and see all 13 root server names returned.
hack.meta: Occasionally tested: 'How many root DNS servers?' = 13 (clusters). The trivia answer.

4.3.f.info:
A TLD (Top-Level Domain) is the right-most label of a domain name before the implied root dot. There are two main categories:

  • gTLDs (generic): .com, .org, .net, .edu, .gov, newer ones like .io, .dev, .ai.
  • ccTLDs (country code): two letters per ISO country code. .us, .uk, .de, .jp, .mx, .ca.

Each TLD is operated by a registry (e.g., Verisign runs .com and .net). The TLD's servers know which authoritative DNS servers are responsible for each second-level domain beneath it.

hack.memory: TLD = the last piece (.com, .uk). gTLD = generic, ccTLD = country. Registries run TLDs.
hack.practice: Run 'whois cisco.com' — see the registry information for the .com TLD.
hack.meta: Light exam coverage — just know TLD = right-most label, gTLD vs ccTLD categories.

4.3.f.info:
An FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name) is the complete, unambiguous name of a host in the DNS hierarchy, including every label up to and including the root. The trailing dot represents the root: www.cisco.com. (with the final dot) is technically an FQDN; www.cisco.com (without) is usually treated as one in practice.

Contrast with a relative hostname like www, which is only meaningful inside a specific domain context (with a search suffix like cisco.com). FQDNs are absolute and globally unique.

On Cisco devices, hostnames in config are often relative (R1) but DNS-based tools use FQDNs (R1.lab.example.com.).

hack.memory: FQDN = full name + trailing dot for root. Absolute, unambiguous. 'www.cisco.com.' vs just 'www'.
hack.practice: In resolv.conf or DNS suffix settings, note how 'web' becomes 'web.example.com.' — that's FQDN completion.
hack.meta: Low weight but often a one-line definition question on the exam.

4.4.a.info:
SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is the standard protocol for monitoring and managing network devices. It allows a central management station to poll routers, switches, firewalls, and servers for operational data (interface counters, CPU, memory, temperature, errors) and to receive unsolicited alerts when events occur.

SNMP is entirely UDP-based — never TCP. It uses two well-known ports: UDP 161 for manager-initiated queries (Get/Set) and UDP 162 for agent-initiated notifications (Trap/Inform).

Three versions exist: v1 (legacy, plaintext community strings), v2c (adds GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters, still plaintext), and v3 (user-based security with optional authentication and encryption). Only v3 provides confidentiality.

hack.memory: SNMP = UDP only. 161 = queries, 162 = traps. v3 = only secure version.
hack.practice: On a lab router, 'snmp-server community public ro' then poll with snmpwalk — see thousands of OIDs returned.
hack.meta: Core SNMP facts tested every exam: UDP, 161/162, v3 = encryption.

4.4.a.info:
The SNMP Manager (also called NMS — Network Management Station) is the centralized software that monitors the network. Examples: SolarWinds, PRTG, Nagios, LibreNMS, Zabbix, Cacti, Grafana (via SNMP exporter).

The manager does two things: (1) polls agents at regular intervals by sending Get/GetNext/GetBulk requests to UDP 161, and (2) listens on UDP 162 for Traps and Informs pushed by agents when events occur.

The manager stores polled data in a time-series database, draws graphs (bandwidth, CPU, errors), evaluates thresholds (alert if CPU > 80%), and can optionally push configuration changes to devices via SNMP Set.

hack.memory: Manager = the boss with the dashboard. Polls agents + listens for alerts.
hack.practice: Install LibreNMS or PRTG free edition at home — add a router as a device — watch it auto-discover via SNMP.
hack.meta: Manager vs Agent is a fundamental distinction. Manager pulls and receives; agent serves and pushes.

4.4.a.info:
The SNMP Agent is the software running ON the managed device (router, switch, server) that answers manager queries and sends unsolicited notifications. On Cisco IOS, the agent is built-in — you enable it with snmp-server community (v2c) or snmp-server user (v3) commands.

The agent listens on UDP 161 for incoming Get/Set requests from the manager and responds with the requested OID values. When a significant event occurs (link down, config change, threshold crossed), the agent initiates a Trap or Inform to the manager on UDP 162.

The agent exposes the device's state through the MIB (Management Information Base) — the hierarchical database that defines every OID the agent can report on.

hack.memory: Agent = on-device software. Listens 161, sends to 162. Talks to manager via MIB.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router, 'snmp-server community lab ro' + 'snmp-server enable traps' — agent is now active.
hack.meta: Every managed device runs an agent. No agent = no SNMP data from that device.

4.4.a.info:
The MIB (Management Information Base) is a hierarchical, tree-structured database defining every manageable object on a device. Each object is identified by a unique OID (Object Identifier) expressed as a dotted-number path, like 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0 (sysUpTime).

The MIB tree starts at iso(1) and descends: iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2.system.sysUpTime.0. Standard MIBs (defined by IETF RFCs) cover universal objects like interfaces (IF-MIB), routing, and system (MIB-II). Vendor-specific MIBs (like CISCO-PROCESS-MIB) cover proprietary features.

Common OIDs: sysDescr (device description), sysUpTime (uptime), ifDescr (interface names), ifInOctets / ifOutOctets (bandwidth counters), ifOperStatus (up/down status).

1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0      = sysDescr
1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0      = sysUpTime
1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10   = ifInOctets

hack.memory: MIB = tree of OIDs. OID = dotted path. sysUpTime = .1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3
hack.practice: 'snmpwalk -v2c -c public [router-IP] 1.3.6.1.2.1' — dumps every MIB-II OID on the device.
hack.meta: Don't memorize OIDs — understand the MIB is a tree of objects the agent exposes.

4.4.a.info:
SNMP uses two UDP ports, and knowing which is which is a near-guaranteed exam question:

  • UDP 161 — Manager-to-Agent queries (Get, GetNext, GetBulk, Set). The agent listens on 161. Think: '161 = the agent answers the door.'
  • UDP 162 — Agent-to-Manager notifications (Trap, Inform). The manager listens on 162. Think: '162 = the manager gets a phone call.'

Memory hook: 161 comes first (lower) = the manager starts the conversation; 162 (higher) = the agent escalates on its own. Both are UDP — SNMP never uses TCP.

hack.memory: 161 = agent side (inbound queries). 162 = manager side (inbound traps). Both UDP. Never TCP.
hack.practice: Run 'show run | include snmp' on a Cisco device — see which community strings are configured.
hack.meta: Expect 1-2 port questions per exam: which port for polling vs trapping.

4.4.b.info:
The Get family of SNMP messages is how the manager pulls data from agents. All three go from manager to agent on UDP 161.

  • Get: Retrieve the value of ONE specific OID. 'Give me sysUpTime.' Returns the exact requested OID.
  • GetNext: Retrieve the OID that comes immediately AFTER the specified one in the MIB tree. Used to walk through the MIB when you don't know the exact OID names. Basis of the snmpwalk utility.
  • GetBulk: Retrieve a large block of consecutive OIDs in a single response. Dramatically more efficient than repeated GetNext calls. Available in SNMPv2c and v3 only — not v1.

hack.memory: Get = one. GetNext = walk one step. GetBulk = dump a range (v2c+).
hack.practice: 'snmpget' = Get. 'snmpwalk' uses GetNext. 'snmpbulkget' = GetBulk. Try each.
hack.meta: GetBulk appeared in v2c — a frequent exam detail.

4.4.b.info:
Set is the write operation — the manager modifies a value on the agent. Set uses UDP 161 and requires read-write access: a RW community string (v2c) or a user with write privileges (v3).

Common uses: shutting down an interface, changing a hostname, rebooting a device, writing config changes, or clearing counters. Many organizations disable SNMP Set entirely for security reasons and only allow read-only SNMP, using SSH/NETCONF for any configuration changes.

Because Set can change device state, it is the single most dangerous SNMP message. A leaked RW community string (especially 'private') is equivalent to an admin login with zero auditing.

hack.memory: Set = WRITE = dangerous = requires RW access. Often disabled entirely.
hack.practice: In a lab, 'snmpset' to shut down an interface. See the real power (and danger) of SNMP Set.
hack.meta: Exam: 'Which SNMP message modifies a device?' = Set. Requires RW access.

4.4.b.info:
A Trap is an unsolicited, unreliable notification sent by the agent to the manager when an event occurs. Traps use UDP 162 and are fire-and-forget — the agent sends once, hopes the manager received it, and moves on. No acknowledgment, no retransmission.

If the Trap packet is lost in transit (network congestion, firewall drop, packet corruption), the manager never knows the event occurred. This is the main weakness of Traps and the reason Informs were added in SNMPv2c.

Despite the weakness, Traps dominate in practice because they consume less bandwidth and agent resources than Informs. Common Traps: link down, link up, authentication failure, cold start (reboot), warm start (reload), config change.

nodes: If lost -> event is never seen

hack.memory: Trap = fire-and-forget. No ACK. If lost, gone forever. Cheap and unreliable.
hack.practice: 'snmp-server enable traps config' on a router. Make a config change. Watch the trap appear at the manager.
hack.meta: Trap vs Inform is THE most common SNMP question. Trap = unreliable.

4.4.b.info:
An Inform is a reliable version of a Trap. The agent sends the Inform on UDP 162, AND the manager must respond with an acknowledgment. If no ACK arrives within a timeout, the agent retransmits the Inform — up to a configurable retry count.

Informs guarantee delivery at the cost of bandwidth and agent state. The agent must remember each outstanding Inform until acknowledged, so there is a memory cost on busy devices.

Informs are v2c and v3 only — not available in v1. You configure them with snmp-server host [ip] informs version 2c [community] instead of the default trap syntax.

hack.memory: Inform = Trap + ACK = reliable. v2c/v3 only. Costs more bandwidth but doesn't lose alerts.
hack.practice: Configure 'snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 informs version 2c lab' — note 'informs' keyword makes it reliable.
hack.meta: Inform = reliable = requires ACK = v2c+. All four facts tied together.

4.4.b.info:
Both Trap and Inform are agent-initiated notifications sent to UDP 162. They differ only in reliability:

  • Trap: fire-and-forget. No ACK, no retransmission. Agent uses less memory but events can be silently lost.
  • Inform: reliable. Manager must ACK; agent retransmits if no ACK. Costs more bandwidth and agent memory.

Key points: (1) Both use UDP 162. (2) Both are sent by the agent. (3) Both can be SNMPv2c or v3 (Inform does not exist in v1). (4) Only Inform is reliable.

Exam answer template: 'Inform is the reliable/acknowledged version of Trap.'

hack.memory: Trap = unreliable. Inform = reliable. Both UDP 162. Both agent-initiated. Inform = v2c+.
hack.practice: Flashcard this exact pairing. It will appear on the exam.
hack.meta: THE single most common SNMP exam question category.

4.4.c.info:
Community strings are SNMPv2c's authentication mechanism — shared-secret passwords in plaintext. The manager includes the community string in every packet; the agent checks it against its configured value and replies only on a match.

There are two access levels:

  • Read-only (RO): allows Get operations only. Cannot modify the device.
  • Read-write (RW): allows both Get and Set. Can modify the device.

Community strings are case-sensitive. 'Public' and 'public' are different strings. Because they travel in the clear, anyone with a packet capture on the network path can read them — which is why SNMPv3 exists.

hack.memory: Community string = plaintext password. RO = look only. RW = look + touch. Case-sensitive.
hack.practice: 'snmp-server community MySecret ro' then poll with wrong case — rejected.
hack.meta: Community strings = v1/v2c hallmark. v3 dropped them entirely.

4.4.c.info:
'public' (read-only) and 'private' (read-write) are the historical default community strings on nearly every SNMP-capable device ever shipped. They are the first strings an attacker tries.

In production, you should NEVER use these defaults. A device with snmp-server community public ro configured is trivially discoverable via SNMP scans, and any RW community string exposes the device to remote reconfiguration.

Best practice: use long random strings (e.g., 20+ characters), rotate them periodically, restrict SNMP access with ACLs (snmp-server community MySecret RO 10), and move to SNMPv3 whenever possible.

hack.memory: public = RO default. private = RW default. NEVER use in prod. First thing an attacker tries.
hack.practice: Run an nmap SNMP scan on a test lab — watch how quickly it finds 'public'.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam recall: public/private are the insecure defaults.

4.4.c.info:
SNMPv1 and SNMPv2c share the same plaintext community-string security model. v2c's 'c' literally stands for 'community' — it kept v1's authentication approach but added operational improvements:

  • GetBulk: efficient retrieval of many OIDs in one message.
  • Inform: reliable (acknowledged) notifications.
  • 64-bit counters: needed for gigabit+ interfaces (v1's 32-bit counters roll over in seconds on 10G links).
  • Better error handling: more specific error codes.

v2c did NOT improve security. Community strings still travel in plaintext. v1 is basically obsolete; v2c remains common in internal networks despite its weaknesses.

hack.memory: v1 -> v2c: added GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters. Security UNCHANGED.
hack.practice: 'show snmp' on a router shows which version is enabled; most Cisco defaults have v1 and v2c both enabled.
hack.meta: Security improvements are v3's job — v2c is just operational upgrades.

4.4.c.info:
snmp-server community [string] [RO|RW] [acl] is the Cisco IOS command that enables SNMPv2c access. It creates a community string and assigns its privilege level in one line.

Best-practice options: attach an ACL to restrict which source IPs can use the string (snmp-server community MySecret RO 10 where ACL 10 permits only the NMS IP). Use view to limit which MIB subtree the community can see.

Router(config)# access-list 10 permit host 10.1.1.100
Router(config)# snmp-server community Mon1t0rR0 RO 10
Router(config)# snmp-server community Mgmt-RW-x7$ RW 10
Router(config)# snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 version 2c Mon1t0rR0

hack.memory: snmp-server community STRING RO|RW ACL. ACL restricts the source IP — always use one.
hack.practice: Configure with and without an ACL; verify only permitted IPs can poll.
hack.meta: Exam config recall. Know the keyword order.

4.4.d.info:
SNMPv3 is the modern, secure version of SNMP. It replaces community strings with a user-based security model (USM): each user has a username, group membership, authentication credentials, and optional privacy (encryption) credentials.

v3 supports three security levels: noAuthNoPriv (username only), authNoPriv (username + hash authentication), and authPriv (username + auth + encryption). Only authPriv provides confidentiality of the SNMP payload.

v3 also adds views — fine-grained access control to specific subtrees of the MIB. You can give a monitoring user read access only to interface counters, while an admin user gets full access. This is impossible with v1/v2c communities.

hack.memory: v3 = user-based + optional auth + optional encryption. Only v3 encrypts SNMP.
hack.practice: Configure a v3 user at authPriv; capture in Wireshark — payload is unreadable ciphertext.
hack.meta: v3 = the only version tested as 'secure.' Know its three levels.

4.4.d.info:
noAuthNoPriv is the weakest SNMPv3 security level. The user is identified by username only — no authentication hash, no encryption. Effectively the same security as v2c community strings, just with a username instead of a 'string.'

Used rarely and only in low-risk scenarios (read-only public-info polling behind trusted network perimeters). Never used when the data or device is sensitive.

Configuration: snmp-server group G1 v3 noauth and snmp-server user U1 G1 v3 (no auth or priv keywords).

hack.memory: noAuthNoPriv = username-only = barely better than v2c. Don't use.
hack.practice: Configure a noAuth user and verify polling works — then realize anyone can spoof the username.
hack.meta: One of three v3 levels. Weakest. Rarely production-appropriate.

4.4.d.info:
authNoPriv adds authentication using an MD5 or SHA hash of the message + auth password. This verifies the sender's identity and ensures the message wasn't modified in transit — but the SNMP payload is still sent in cleartext.

Good for networks where spoofing prevention is needed but data confidentiality is not a concern (e.g., polling non-sensitive interface counters inside a trusted segment). SHA is preferred over MD5 — SHA is cryptographically stronger, though both are showing their age.

Configuration: snmp-server group G1 v3 auth and snmp-server user U1 G1 v3 auth sha MyAuthPass.

hack.memory: authNoPriv = signed letter in a clear envelope. Sender verified, contents visible.
hack.practice: Capture authNoPriv traffic — see the HMAC auth field but plaintext OID values.
hack.meta: Middle v3 level. Auth yes, encryption no. Know the distinction from authPriv.

4.4.d.info:
authPriv is the strongest SNMPv3 security level: it provides both authentication AND encryption (privacy). Authentication uses MD5 or SHA; encryption uses DES, 3DES, or AES (AES-128/192/256).

This is the ONLY SNMP configuration that provides confidentiality. All other levels (v1, v2c, v3 noAuthNoPriv, v3 authNoPriv) send SNMP data in plaintext.

Configuration example (SHA auth + AES-128 encryption):

snmp-server group Admins v3 priv
snmp-server user NetAdmin Admins v3 auth sha MyAuthPass priv aes 128 MyPrivPass

hack.memory: authPriv = the ONLY SNMP level that encrypts. auth=MD5/SHA, priv=DES/3DES/AES.
hack.practice: Capture authPriv traffic in Wireshark — payload is unreadable ciphertext.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question: 'Which level provides auth AND encryption?' = authPriv.

4.4.d.info:
SNMPv3 uses two categories of cryptographic algorithms:

  • Authentication (auth): MD5 or SHA. SHA is preferred — MD5 is cryptographically broken for some use cases. Both produce an HMAC-style hash for message integrity and sender verification.
  • Privacy (priv, encryption): DES (56-bit, weak, deprecated), 3DES (168-bit, slow but available), or AES at 128/192/256 bits. AES is the modern standard and preferred choice.

Cisco IOS defaults vary by platform and version, but modern best practice is auth sha + priv aes 128 (or higher).

hack.memory: Auth: MD5 or SHA (SHA better). Privacy: DES/3DES/AES (AES best).
hack.practice: Configure v3 with each algorithm combination and note command differences.
hack.meta: Know the algorithm families for auth vs priv.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv1 has essentially no security. It uses plaintext community strings as its sole authentication mechanism. There is no hashing, no encryption, no user-based model, and no integrity checking.

Anyone with a packet capture on the path can read the community string and immediately gain equivalent SNMP access. v1 is considered obsolete and is only found on very old devices or legacy management systems.

v1 also lacks operational improvements: no GetBulk, no Inform, only 32-bit counters (which overflow in seconds on gigabit+ interfaces).

hack.memory: v1 = plaintext + nothing else. Obsolete. Don't deploy.
hack.practice: Look at 'show snmp' on a legacy device; v1 may still be enabled — disable it.
hack.meta: Exam-level detail: v1 is the oldest, weakest, mostly deprecated.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv2c uses the same plaintext community string model as v1 — no security improvements whatsoever. The 'c' in v2c stands for 'community.' What v2c adds are OPERATIONAL improvements: GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters, and better error codes.

Because of its simplicity and lack of key-management overhead, v2c is STILL the most widely deployed SNMP version in internal networks, despite its security weaknesses. Many organizations accept the risk because their management traffic flows over a trusted out-of-band network segment.

hack.memory: v2c = v1 security + better messaging. Still plaintext. Most widely deployed in the real world.
hack.practice: Capture v2c traffic — community string appears in clear, right in the packet bytes.
hack.meta: 'Same security as v1' is the key test point for v2c.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv3 introduced the User-based Security Model (USM), completely replacing community strings. Each SNMP user is defined on the agent with a username, authentication password (MD5/SHA), and optional privacy password (DES/3DES/AES).

Three levels of security are available: noAuthNoPriv (username only), authNoPriv (username + hash auth), and authPriv (username + auth + encryption). authPriv is the only level providing confidentiality.

v3 also adds view-based access control (VACM) — fine-grained restriction of which MIB subtrees a user can read or write. Combined with USM, v3 provides authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and authorization.

hack.memory: v3 = users + groups + 3 levels + views. Only v3 = real security.
hack.practice: Configure a v3 user at authPriv — note the multi-line command vs v2c's one-liner.
hack.meta: v3 = the 'secure SNMP' answer. Memorize: USM, three levels, views.

4.4.e.info:
Only SNMPv3 at the authPriv level provides encryption. Every other combination transmits SNMP data in plaintext:

  • SNMPv1: plaintext (no auth, no encryption).
  • SNMPv2c: plaintext (no auth, no encryption).
  • SNMPv3 noAuthNoPriv: plaintext (username only, no encryption).
  • SNMPv3 authNoPriv: plaintext payload (auth hash but no encryption).
  • SNMPv3 authPriv: ENCRYPTED (auth + DES/3DES/AES encryption).

This is the #1 SNMP security question on the CCNA. Pattern: 'Which SNMP version/level provides encryption?' -> 'SNMPv3, authPriv.'

hack.memory: Only v3 authPriv encrypts. Literally one answer. Drill until instant.
hack.practice: Write this on a flashcard: 'authPriv = only SNMP option that encrypts.' Review daily.
hack.meta: Most-tested SNMP question. Non-negotiable recall.
